old_claim.note: Commercial Aviation Systems provides a capability that enables this technology without being the only possible path.

new_claim.note: The first jet airliner milestone is scheduled passenger service by BOAC, so commercial airline operations are deployment context rather than the propulsion technology itself.

source_shape.source_locator: Prevailing Cultural / Organizational Factors section describing the Comet's May 2, 1952 inaugural civilian jet transport flight.
source_shape.source_claim_summary: The FAA account anchors the jet-airliner milestone to BOAC's civilian passenger operation of the de Havilland Comet rather than to the earlier existence of jet aircraft alone.
source_shape.source_support_rationale: Because the node represents commercial passenger jetliners, the airline-service setting is a deployment and scaling dependency; the aircraft propulsion capability remains the technical requirement.

ontology_before: Commercial aviation was modeled as a generic enabling dependency for jet airliners.
ontology_after: Commercial aviation is modeled as deployment and scaling context, while jet aircraft capability remains the direct category requirement.
invariant_preserved_or_changed: Graph invariant 2026-06-09-transport-drilling-materials-chronology.json locks this commercial_or_scaling_dependency edge type.
would_reject_if: The node were narrowed to prototype jet passenger aircraft rather than commercial airline service.
A better source established a non-airline commercial deployment milestone as the first jet airliner.
The graph split jet-airliner design from airline operation and moved commercial service to a separate deployment node.
short_reason: Commercial aviation is the deployment system for jet airliners, not the propulsion technology.
